content:
[S1]: Antibiotic resistance is a big problem that's happening all around the world, and it's making a lot of people sick.
[READER]: I'm a little curious, but I'm not really sure what "antibiotic resistance" means - it sounds like a big phrase. I want to know what it means and why it's a problem. The next sentence should explain what antibiotic resistance is in a way that's easy to understand.

[S2]: When we take antibiotics, they help kill the bad germs that make us sick, but sometimes those germs can become super strong and the antibiotics don't work anymore.
[READER]: Oh, I think I get it - it's like the germs are getting stronger and the medicine can't hurt them. But why is this happening now, and not before? I want to know why this is a new problem. The next sentence should tell me why this is happening more often now.

[S3]: One reason this is happening is that people are taking antibiotics too much, even when they're not really needed, like when they have a cold or the flu.
[READER]: Hmm, my mom always says I need to finish my medicine, but now you're saying that taking too much medicine is bad? I'm a little confused. The next sentence should explain why taking too much medicine is a problem.

[S4]: When we take antibiotics too much, it helps the strong germs grow and multiply, making them even stronger and harder to kill.
[READER]: Okay, that makes sense, I think. But what about other people - are they also taking too much medicine? Is that why this is a big problem everywhere? The next sentence should tell me if this is a problem that lots of people are contributing to.

[S5]: Yes, lots of people all around the world are taking antibiotics too much, and that's making the problem of antibiotic resistance spread really fast.
[READER]: Whoa, that's scary - it sounds like a big mess! What can we do to stop it? I want to know if there's something I can do to help. The next sentence should give me some hope and tell me what I can do.

[S6]: We can all help by only taking antibiotics when we really need them, and by making sure to finish the whole dose like our doctor tells us to.
[READER]: Okay, that sounds like a good plan - I can do that! But what about the really sick people - will they be okay? The next sentence should reassure me that doctors are working on a solution.

[S7]: Doctors and scientists are working really hard to find new ways to kill the super strong germs and to make new antibiotics that will work.
[READER]: That's good to know - it makes me feel a little better. But I'm still worried - what if they can't find a new medicine in time? The next sentence should tell me that there's still hope and that we can all work together to solve this problem.

[S8]: Even if it takes a while, if we all do our part and are careful with antibiotics, we can help slow down the spread of antibiotic resistance and keep people from getting really sick.
[READER]: Okay, I feel a little better now - I think I can help make a difference. I'm glad I learned about this, and I want to tell my friends and family to be careful with antibiotics too! The next sentence should summarize what I learned and encourage me to share what I know.
